includedPullRequests.title: Fix temp target presets showing up in the main chart
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes #1324

Saving a new Temp Target as a preset made it show up in the main chart even though it was never activated. The chart's fetch predicate freezes its date anchors when it is built and only re-anchors when the app comes to the foreground, so a fresh preset (date now, enabled false) matched the clause meant for scheduled temp targets. The same stale anchor also kept a cancelled temp target's full bar on the chart until the app was backgrounded.

Presets are now excluded from the scheduled clause, and the fetched results are filtered again with a fresh date before they reach the chart. Added tests covering the predicate and the visibility rules.

includedPullRequests.title: Make bolus-in-progress card in Treatments view more reliable
includedPullRequests.body:
Some manual boluses left a bolusing card stuck at 0% in the Treatments view that blocked further boluses and could not be dismissed until the app was restarted (#1308).

The card was gated on bolusStatus, which mirrors the pump's reported bolusState and can stay at inProgress after delivery has finished. Since bolusTrigger now retains that value, the card resurfaced once a new amount was entered, and the X could not clear it because there was no active bolus to cancel.

Gate the card on bolusProgress instead, the local dose reporter signal that clears reliably and that the Home view already uses, keeping the initiating state so the pre-delivery view still shows.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix duplicate Nightscout treatment uploads by serializing per-pipeline runs
includedPullRequests.body:
## Problem

Trio occasionally uploads the same treatment to Nightscout twice — most visibly Exercise entries for overrides enacted via Shortcuts or remote commands. Reproduced with an APNS remote command producing two documents with millisecond-identical `created_at` and consecutive Mongo `_id`s, i.e. two near-simultaneous POSTs:

- Enacting an override via App Intents / remote control triggers uploads twice: the Core Data save fires the upload pipeline, and `.willUpdateOverrideConfiguration` fires a direct, unthrottled upload. Both fetch the same rows still marked `isUploadedToNS == false` before either has marked them uploaded.
- Nightscout's treatment upsert is a non-atomic find-then-insert (no unique index), so concurrent identical POSTs both insert. Sequential re-posts are deduped server-side; only concurrent ones slip through. (API v3 has the same race, so switching API versions wouldn't help.)
- The 2s throttle didn't prevent this: it only spaced out task starts within one trigger path, never waited for the previous upload to finish, and with `latest: false` silently dropped triggers arriving inside its window. Carb edits had a third, entirely unthrottled path (`HistoryStateModel` calls `uploadCarbs()` directly).

## Solution

Replace the per-pipeline subject/throttle machinery with `NightscoutUploadSerializer`, an actor that coalesces and serializes upload runs per pipeline:

- An idle pipeline starts a run immediately; while a run is in flight, exactly one follow-up is queued and further requests coalesce into it.
- No two runs of a pipeline ever overlap: a follow-up re-fetches only after the previous run marked its rows uploaded, so it finds nothing left to send. Queued runs double as retry attempts after network failures.
- The upload routine is provided once at init; `request(_:)`/`run(_:)` take only the pipeline, so every entry point (Core Data triggers, App Intents, remote control, direct calls) is covered with no call-site changes.
- App Intents semantics are preserved: `uploadOverrides()`/`uploadTempTargets()` are awaitable, so `.didUpdateOverrideConfiguration` still posts only after the upload attempt finished.

## Benefits

- No more duplicate treatments, regardless of how many trigger paths fire for the same change.
- Uploads start immediately — the fixed 2-second throttle delay is gone.
- No dropped triggers (the old throttle discarded a second change arriving within 2 s; it stayed unuploaded until the next unrelated trigger).
- `uploadDeviceStatus()` no longer throws; its only caller was the deleted pipeline dispatch, and errors are logged in `performUpload(for:)`.

Unit tests cover the serializer: no overlap under concurrency, queued-run ordering, burst coalescing into a single follow-up, completion semantics, and pipeline independence.

includedPullRequests.title: [Part 5 of 6] Remove JSON encoding for pump events
includedPullRequests.body:
This commit removes the old:
* CoreData -> DTOs -> JSON string -> [PumpHistoryEvent]

pipeline and replaces it with a direct pipeline:
* CoreData -> [PumpHistoryEvent]

The result of the two pipelines should be identical, except for the
handling of the subtype tempType of temp basal events, which we
nullify if we get an unrecognized string vs crashing as the old
pipeline would. This change is safe as Trio only ever records
"absolute" types and the value is copied but never actually used by
the algorithm.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Temp Target and Override delete confirmation auto-dismissing
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes #1314

Deleting a Temp Target or Override preset by swiping was unreliable. The delete confirmation only appeared for a moment before auto-dismissing, so the preset often couldn't be deleted.

The confirmation dialog was attached to the ForEach inside the List and driven by two state variables set at the same time, which made it dismiss itself. Reworked it to match the History screen: a single optional drives the dialog via presenting:, attached once to the view. Applied to both Temp Targets and Overrides.

includedPullRequests.title: [Part 6 of 6] Remove OpenAPSSwift.swift and use native arguments
includedPullRequests.body:
This commit is the last part of our removal of the JSON conversion we
used to do when we ran the oref algorithm using Javascript. In this
commit, we remove the OpenAPSSwift file and just call the underlying
algorithm functions directly from OpenAPS.

One change in functionality is that we expose raw DeterminationError
messages instead of catching them and converting them to APSError
exceptions with a generic "No determination error" error
message. These exception messages will be user visible when they
happen.

includedPullRequests.title: Remove explicit allowsConstrainedNetworkAccess setting for Nightscout uploads
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

This PR removes the explicit `allowsConstrainedNetworkAccess` setting from all Nightscout upload requests.

This restores the default `URLRequest` behavior and lets iOS decide how constrained network access should be handled according to the user's system settings.

## Background

While investigating intermittent Nightscout upload failures, repeated `Constrained path prohibited` errors were observed in the logs. This PR follows the review discussion and removes the explicit flag instead of overriding the system default.

includedPullRequests.title: Core Data Performance Improvements, Fixes and Adjustments
includedPullRequests.body:
**Core Data Context Rework**
Eliminates long-lived shared `NSManagedObjectContext` instances across all storages and service classes which was the reason for inconsistencies in the synchronization of our background contexts and probably also the root cause for the crash that was solved differently with one of my previous PRs #1100. Each data operation now creates a fresh task context via `CoreDataStack.shared.newTaskContext()`, preventing cross-context data races and stale object access. Every background context now is only active for a special task and it has the latest data from the `PersistentStore`. This is done similarly in Apples Earthquakes Sample: https://github.com/TomaszLizer/Apple-Earthquakes/

Those changes need to be tested thoroughly. I am testing it on myself for about 2 months now without any problems. But in theory I could have accidentally introduced a wrong `NSManagedObjectContext` access in a class that I simply don't use. This should be deteced via our `ConcurrencyDebug` flag though which then makes the app crash. So be aware of that.

 **N+1 Query Elimination**
 Adds `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` for bolus/tempBasal on all `PumpEventStored` fetches (`OpenAPS`, `APSManager`, `WatchManager`, `GarminManager`)and `forecastValues` on `OrefDetermination` fetches. Eliminates hundreds of individual `WHERE Z_PK = ?` round-trips per Loop that I could identify in the SQLDebug logs. When we were fetching a pump event with its bolus relationship for example the relationships don't get loaded automatically, but instead created as a fault. So when we were doing 1 query only to iterate over the items to get its bolus property after that this was unefficient as this would fire several faults. `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` gets rid of this as the bolus property is now already in the context cache after the first query.

**TDD Aggregate Optimization**
Replaces full ZTDDStored row loads (up to 1,041 rows) in `calculateWeightedAverage` with a single SQL aggregate query `(SELECT total(), COUNT())` — reducing fetch time from ~6 ms to ~0.8 ms per call.

**Carb/FPU & Determination Prefetch**
 Adds `SELF IN` batch-prefetch hop for CarbEntryStored and OrefDetermination in the Home state model to prevent N+1 faults during `getNSManagedObject` materialization on the view context.

**NSFetchedResultsController migration**
New NSFetchedResultsControllerDelegate plus migration of HomeStateModel, TreatmentsStateModel, NightscoutManager, TidepoolManager, and HealthKitManager to FRCs bound to the viewContext, replacing the previous objectID/publisher logic.

**History-sourced change feed (CoreDataObserver.swift)**
The hand-rolled `changedObjectsOnManagedObjectContextDidSavePublisher` (based on `NSManagedObjectContextDidSave`) is replaced by `CoreDataStack.entityChangePublisher`, sourced from persistent history. This now also captures batch operations and cross-process changes.

**New dedup logic in `PumpHistoryStorage`**
The new dedup key combines `timestamp` AND `type` so that a bolus and temp basal event can coexist at the same time. Also add a unique constraint that ensures this behaviour.

includedPullRequests.title: Core Data Performance Improvements, Fixes and Adjustments
includedPullRequests.body:
**Core Data Context Rework**
Eliminates long-lived shared `NSManagedObjectContext` instances across all storages and service classes which was the reason for inconsistencies in the synchronization of our background contexts and probably also the root cause for the crash that was solved differently with one of my previous PRs #1100. Each data operation now creates a fresh task context via `CoreDataStack.shared.newTaskContext()`, preventing cross-context data races and stale object access. Every background context now is only active for a special task and it has the latest data from the `PersistentStore`. This is done similarly in Apples Earthquakes Sample: https://github.com/TomaszLizer/Apple-Earthquakes/

Those changes need to be tested thoroughly. I am testing it on myself for about 2 months now without any problems. But in theory I could have accidentally introduced a wrong `NSManagedObjectContext` access in a class that I simply don't use. This should be deteced via our `ConcurrencyDebug` flag though which then makes the app crash. So be aware of that.

 **N+1 Query Elimination**
 Adds `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` for bolus/tempBasal on all `PumpEventStored` fetches (`OpenAPS`, `APSManager`, `WatchManager`, `GarminManager`)and `forecastValues` on `OrefDetermination` fetches. Eliminates hundreds of individual `WHERE Z_PK = ?` round-trips per Loop that I could identify in the SQLDebug logs. When we were fetching a pump event with its bolus relationship for example the relationships don't get loaded automatically, but instead created as a fault. So when we were doing 1 query only to iterate over the items to get its bolus property after that this was unefficient as this would fire several faults. `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` gets rid of this as the bolus property is now already in the context cache after the first query.

**TDD Aggregate Optimization**
Replaces full ZTDDStored row loads (up to 1,041 rows) in `calculateWeightedAverage` with a single SQL aggregate query `(SELECT total(), COUNT())` — reducing fetch time from ~6 ms to ~0.8 ms per call.

**Carb/FPU & Determination Prefetch**
 Adds `SELF IN` batch-prefetch hop for CarbEntryStored and OrefDetermination in the Home state model to prevent N+1 faults during `getNSManagedObject` materialization on the view context.

**NSFetchedResultsController migration**
New NSFetchedResultsControllerDelegate plus migration of HomeStateModel, TreatmentsStateModel, NightscoutManager, TidepoolManager, and HealthKitManager to FRCs bound to the viewContext, replacing the previous objectID/publisher logic.

**History-sourced change feed (CoreDataObserver.swift)**
The hand-rolled `changedObjectsOnManagedObjectContextDidSavePublisher` (based on `NSManagedObjectContextDidSave`) is replaced by `CoreDataStack.entityChangePublisher`, sourced from persistent history. This now also captures batch operations and cross-process changes.

**New dedup logic in `PumpHistoryStorage`**
The new dedup key combines `timestamp` AND `type` so that a bolus and temp basal event can coexist at the same time. Also add a unique constraint that ensures this behaviour.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Spurious Pump Alerts
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

The alerting rework routes pump-thrown alerts through the LoopKit protocol as intended. Tapping a notification calls acknowledgeAlert on the PM, no auto-ack anywhere. But it missed one spot: dismissing the in-app banner (and swiping away the UN) routed to snooze only, so the ack was never sent to the PM. With the app foregrounded, a pod alert could stay unacknowledged on the pod indefinitely unless the user also tapped the notification, leaving pods beeping on their repeat schedule (15 min expiration reminder, hourly low reservoir/expired) and alerts stuck as unacknowledged.

Dismissal is the user's response, so requestSnooze now also forwards the ack to the issuing device manager via acknowledgeOnDevice(), extracted from handleAcknowledgement. The forward is guarded on a registered AlertResponder – only device managers register (currently just the pump manager) – so Trio-internal alerts (glucose for Libre 3 and other CGms that require it, not-looping, algorithm) are unaffected, and PMs with no-op ack's (Medtrum, Dana, Minimed) see no behavior change.

User-facing behavior is unchanged: swipe/tap away still auto-snoozes for 15 min and dismisses both presentations, **but** pump alerts are now properly acknowledged at the PM layer (e.g. AcknowledgeAlerts reaches the pod and silences its beeps).

Fixes #1135.

includedPullRequests.title: Fix spurious 'Bolus failed' notification after manual bolus
includedPullRequests.body:
fixes #1150 
The post-bolus determineBasalSync() call was inside the bolus do/catch, so any algorithm error (e.g. 'Not enough glucose data' during CGM warm-up) was misreported as a bolus failure even though the pump had already delivered the bolus successfully. Wrap the algorithm refresh in its own do/catch so it cannot trigger bolusDidFail or a pumpError notification.

includedPullRequests.title: Trio v0.8.1 patch release
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Rolls three recently-landed tracks into a patch release: the new OmnipodKit pump manager and a security-driven dependency bump.

## What's Included

  1. OmnipodKit pump manager (Eros + DASH unified) — adds loopandlearn/OmnipodKit as a new submodule plus the wiring to surface it in the Add Pump flow as "All Omnipod Types". Pod type (Eros vs. DASH) is selected at activation rather than at the pump-manager level. OmniKit and OmniBLE remain in place unchanged — existing users on those drivers see no behavior change; the new driver is purely opt-in until a user explicitly selects it on next pod start.

  2. Security: fastlane 2.235.0 / jwt 3.2.0 — bumps fastlane 2.233.1 → 2.235.0, which relaxes its transitive jwt < 3 cap and lets the resolver land jwt 3.2.0+. Closes Dependabot alert #14 (ruby-jwt empty-key HMAC bypass, sibling of CVE-2026-44351).
  CI/release tooling only, no shipped-app code surface change. 

  3. Fix Telemetry Daily Sends — continuous background pings were not being correctly throttled to once per day. The daily send logic is now enforced properly. 

It also includes small fixes added recently to dev:
* Fix Spurious "Bolus Failed" Notification After Manual Bolus (https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1151)
* Telemetry Payload Improvements (#1172)

includedPullRequests.title: Update telemetry payload: fix buildDate format, add locale/TZ/appName
includedPullRequests.body:
Fixes a few issues identified with the Trio telemetry payloads:

* On non-English locales, the buildDate was improperly formatted. e.g., a builder on a French language-default OS would emit `lun. mai 2`. Fix: force capture-build-details to POSIX default locale (english).
* Parse the timestamp and emit as YYYY-MM-DD instead of “Tue May 26” format
* Add iOS system locale + timezone to telemetry, with the above bug as motivation for why we should track this. Plus, this will be beneficial for users when managing translations.
* Add appName to telemetry -- hardcoded as "Trio", but can be overridden for forks

includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Rolls three recently-landed tracks into a patch release: the new OmnipodKit pump manager and a security-driven dependency bump.

## What's Included

  1. OmnipodKit pump manager (Eros + DASH unified) — adds loopandlearn/OmnipodKit as a new submodule plus the wiring to surface it in the Add Pump flow as "All Omnipod Types". Pod type (Eros vs. DASH) is selected at activation rather than at the pump-manager level. OmniKit and OmniBLE remain in place unchanged — existing users on those drivers see no behavior change; the new driver is purely opt-in until a user explicitly selects it on next pod start.

  2. Security: fastlane 2.235.0 / jwt 3.2.0 — bumps fastlane 2.233.1 → 2.235.0, which relaxes its transitive jwt < 3 cap and lets the resolver land jwt 3.2.0+. Closes Dependabot alert #14 (ruby-jwt empty-key HMAC bypass, sibling of CVE-2026-44351).
  CI/release tooling only, no shipped-app code surface change. 

  3. Fix Telemetry Daily Sends — continuous background pings were not being correctly throttled to once per day. The daily send logic is now enforced properly. 

It also includes small fixes added recently to dev:
* Fix Spurious "Bolus Failed" Notification After Manual Bolus (https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/pull/1151)
* Telemetry Payload Improvements (#1172)

includedPullRequests.body:
**For detailed release notes, see: https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/releases/tag/v0.8.0**

## What's Changed At A Glance

* **Anonymous opt-out Telemetry**: helps the team understand how Trio is used, backed by Apple App Attest
* **Therapy settings upload to Tidepool**: Trio now pushes basal, CR, ISF, and target settings into Tidepool alongside treatment data
* **Delete glucose from Nightscout**: glucose entries deleted in Trio now propagate to Nightscout
* **Override confirmation dialogs**: optional confirmation step before activating an override or temporary target
* **Bolus progress in Treatments view**: see the active bolus and cancel it without leaving the Treatments screen
* **Context menus** on all swipeable history list items (long-press alternative to swipe)
* **Settings Search scroll-to and highlight**: tapping a search result now scrolls to and briefly highlights the matched row
* **Bolus safety checks unified**: Shortcut-driven boluses now get the same max-bolus, max-IOB, and recent-bolus validation as APNS remote boluses
* **Nightscout site change events corrected**: fixed false positives for Medtronic, added a proper `SiteChange` event for Trio History
* **Glucose smoothing window fix**: smoothing now correctly uses the *newest* 350 readings, not the oldest

includedPullRequests.title: Add scroll-to and highlight for settings search results
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Adds scroll-to and highlight behavior when tapping a settings search result. Previously, search navigated to the correct screen but left the user to find the setting themselves. Now it scrolls directly to the setting and briefly highlights it.

This is the first PR of what will I'm sure be several. This contains the core logic and enables it for Algorithm > Dynamic Settings. Remaining screens will follow in subsequent PRs (many will add a single `.settingsHighlightScroll()` line per view).

## How it works

Two view modifiers drive the feature:

- **`.settingsHighlightScroll()`** Added once per destination view. Wraps the view in a `ScrollViewReader` and watches for a highlighted setting, then scrolls to it.
- **`.settingsSearchTarget(label:)`** Added to each setting row. Sets `.id(label)` so `ScrollViewReader` can find it, and adds a highlight animation that flashes when the label matches.

`SettingInputSection` (the shared component used by most settings) applies `.settingsSearchTarget(label:)` automatically using its existing `label` parameter. Most screens will only need a single `.settingsHighlightScroll()` line to opt in. Custom sections (like the Dynamic ISF picker) will need a `.settingsSearchTarget(label:)` added manually.

The search result list now uses `NavigationLink(value: SearchResultTarget(...))` which carries both the destination screen and the scroll target label. On navigation, the label is passed to `SettingsSearchHighlight` (an `@Observable` object in the environment), which triggers the scroll and highlight.

`scrollTargetLabels` on `SettingItem` handles cases where the search term differs from the UI label (e.g. searching "Max IOB" scrolls to "Maximum Insulin on Board (IOB)"). This mapping is data-only and has no effect until a screen opts-in with `.settingsHighlightScroll()`.

## Demo

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e8743199-cb06-431f-afb3-48f92045a9de

## Scroll demo on smaller screen.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/51a5d7b0-7dc7-4a84-b5fd-45dbc6183215

includedPullRequests.title: Settings Search with Scroll-to and Highlight for the Matched Result
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary
Settings search used to surface the right sub-screen but drop you at the top, leaving users to scan for the setting that
actually matched. This PR makes search results jump directly to the matched control and highlight it briefly.

##  Changes
  - New `SettingsSearchHighlight.swift` view modifier (`.settingsHighlightScroll(…)`) that pairs a settings row with the
  search target, scrolls to it on tap-through, and animates a transient highlight.
  - Modifier applied to 16 settings sub-screens so every searchable surface participates.
  - `scrollTargetLabels` mappings on each `SettingItem` for cases where the search hit isn't itself a visible row, e.g.,
  "Carbs Required Threshold" → "Show Carbs Required Badge" (the threshold field only renders when the badge is enabled),
  "High Glucose Target" → "Enable SMB With High Glucose", Bolus Calculator conditional fields, etc. Keeps the user-typed
  term as the entry point while routing to the visible parent.
  - Introduced `SettingsSearchTests.swift` unit coverage for the mapping resolution

Addresses https://github.com/nightscout/Trio/issues/1095
Authored by @gordolio.

includedPullRequests.title: Add scroll targets and fix search index labels for settings search
includedPullRequests.body:
Part 3 of #1095 

- Replace .listRowBackground(Color.chart) with .settingsSearchTarget(label:) on 7 User Interface sections so they become scroll targets for search
- Add .settingsHighlightScroll() to User Interface screen
- Add scrollTargetLabels mappings for Units/Limits, SMB, Target Behavior, Additionals, and User interface
- Fix incorrect searchContents in Bolus Calculator and User Interface

## About `scrollTargetLabels`
`scrollTargetLabels` maps the search term to the actual UI label:
```swift
scrollTargetLabels: [
    "Max IOB": "Maximum Insulin on Board (IOB)",
    "Max Bolus": "Maximum Bolus",
]
```

There are two cases where these are needed:
1. Abbreviated search terms - the search result/index uses a shorter/common name but the label within the UI shows the full name (e.g. "Max Basal" → "Maximum Basal Rate")
2. Multiple search terms targeting one section - several terms scroll to the same UI element
  - "Dark Mode", "Light Mode", and "Appearance" all scroll to the Appearance picker
  - "Cone" and "Lines" both scroll to the "Forecast Display Type"

includedPullRequests.title: Add scroll targets and fix search index labels for settings search
includedPullRequests.body:
Part 3 of #1095 

- Replace .listRowBackground(Color.chart) with .settingsSearchTarget(label:) on 7 User Interface sections so they become scroll targets for search
- Add .settingsHighlightScroll() to User Interface screen
- Add scrollTargetLabels mappings for Units/Limits, SMB, Target Behavior, Additionals, and User interface
- Fix incorrect searchContents in Bolus Calculator and User Interface

## About `scrollTargetLabels`
`scrollTargetLabels` maps the search term to the actual UI label:
```swift
scrollTargetLabels: [
    "Max IOB": "Maximum Insulin on Board (IOB)",
    "Max Bolus": "Maximum Bolus",
]
```

There are two cases where these are needed:
1. Abbreviated search terms - the search result/index uses a shorter/common name but the label within the UI shows the full name (e.g. "Max Basal" → "Maximum Basal Rate")
2. Multiple search terms targeting one section - several terms scroll to the same UI element
  - "Dark Mode", "Light Mode", and "Appearance" all scroll to the Appearance picker
  - "Cone" and "Lines" both scroll to the "Forecast Display Type"

includedPullRequests.title: Shared BolusSafetyValidator for APNS and Shortcut boluses
includedPullRequests.body:
## Summary

Extracts a shared `BolusSafetyValidator` service that centralises the safety checks previously inlined in the APNS remote-bolus path, and applies them to Shortcut-driven boluses as well.

**Checks applied:** max bolus, max IOB, recent-bolus 20% threshold.

## Remote commands (APNS)

Behaviour is unchanged. `TrioRemoteControl+Bolus` now calls the validator with `lookbackStart: Date(timeIntervalSince1970: payload.timestamp)`, so the 20% recent-bolus check still covers the interval between the command being sent and now, and existing rejection messages are preserved. (Minor side-effect: an unavailable IOB now rejects with a logged message instead of throwing.)

## Shortcuts

Shortcut boluses previously checked only the "max bolus" limit. They now get the same full set of checks. Because the Shortcut has no caller-provided origin timestamp, the recent-bolus check uses a 6-minute rolling window (`BolusSafetyEvaluator.recentBolusWindowMinutes`).

Closes #531

includedPullRequests.title: Fix Apple Health flag by checking for sample type permission
includedPullRequests.body:
A tester reported appleHealthEnabled: false despite a working Apple Health integration.

Cause: `hasGrantedFullWritePermissions` required all five Trio-managed sample types (glucose, insulin, carbs, fat, protein) to be authorized.

Changes in `TelemetryClient.buildPayload`: 
- `appleHealthEnabled` → true when any write permission is granted (matches the state-based nightscoutPaired /
  tidepoolPaired pattern already in place).
- New `appleHealthWrites: { glucose, insulin, carbs, fat, protein }`check per-type authorization snapshot. Reuses the existing `checkWriteToHealthPermissions` of HealthKitManager.

highlights.title: Show small basal rates with more precision
changes: Basal rates (units/hour) in the therapy settings editor now show two to three decimal places for small steps.
This increased precision appears during onboarding when editing basal settings.
Displays for other unit types remain unchanged.

highlights.title: Add anonymous telemetry with consent options
changes: Trio now sends optional anonymous usage telemetry with a default opt-out (Full Sharing) choice.
App Diagnostics and Onboarding show three options: Full Sharing (crashes + telemetry), Crash Reports Only, and Disabled.
Existing users see a one-time migration sheet on first launch to choose their telemetry preference.
Settings includes a "What's sent" preview so you can inspect the exact telemetry JSON before consenting.

includedPullRequests.title: Add Anonymous Telemetry
includedPullRequests.body: ## Overview * Adds an opt-out anonymous usage check-in modeled on LoopFollow#626 * Diagnostics consent now has three states: Full Sharing (crashes + telemetry), Crash Reports Only, and Disabled. Default is Full Sharing (opt-out) * Existing Crashlytics wiring is unchanged * Add new flag * Onboarding and Settings → App Diagnostics show the new selection option * Existing users get a one-time migration sheet on first foreground. * New TelemetryClient lives under Services/Telemetry - `TelemetryAttestor` owns `DCAppAttestService` key generation (Secure Enclave-backed), Keychain-persisted registration, and per-ping assertion building. - `TelemetryClient.send()` skips on `DCAppAttestService.isSupported == false` (simulator), short-circuits on a sticky "forbidden" flag set by a 403 from `/api/attest/register`, and posts to `/checkin` with the three `X-AppAttest-*` headers. Assertion is built over `SHA256(payload || challenge)` per the server spec. - `app_id` (`TEAMID.bundle.id`) is read from the embedded `embedded.mobileprovision` file. The `SecTask*` APIs aren't Swift-exposed on iOS; this is the standard workaround. Trio always ships with an embedded mobileprovision (sideload / TestFlight), so this path is reliable. - Added the `com.apple.developer.devicecheck.appattest-environment = development` entitlement (matching the existing `aps-environment`). * Extended PRIVACY_POLICY.md with Telemetry section This PR is still draft until the server side is reviewed in tandem. ## Screenshots | Onboarding | Migration Popup | Settings | |--------|--------|--------| | | | | ## TODOs - [x] Update endpoint once telemetry backend is set up - [x] Test check-in pings and upload end-to-end

includedPullRequests.title: Fix help buttons on Dynamic Settings page
includedPullRequests.body:
The question mark buttons for Adjustment Factor, Weighted Average of TDD, and Adjust Basal on the Dynamic Settings page did nothing when tapped. Only the Dynamic Insulin Sensitivity one worked.

Regression from the cold-tap help-sheet fix. That commit replaced three `@State` vars with a single `HintPayload?` and a derived `shouldDisplayHintBinding`, but the binding's setter only handled `false`. `SettingInputSection` toggles `shouldDisplayHint` from false to true and then assigns the verbose hint based on the new value — since the toggle to `true` was dropped, the next line read `false` and assigned `nil`, so nothing was presented.

Fix lets the setter handle `true` by seeding a placeholder payload. The verbose-hint binding then immediately replaces it with the real label and content on the same action.
